levels.title: Identification & Form
levels.miniLesson:
🎯 Level 1: The Dependent Clause

Subordination uses Subordinating Conjunctions to join a dependent clause to an independent one. The most important rule is the Comma Rule:

  • ➡️ Signal at the Start: [Subordinator] + [Clause], + [Main Clause].
    Because it rained, we stayed home.
  • ➡️ Signal in the Middle: [Main Clause] + [Subordinator] + [Clause].
    We stayed home because it rained.

questionBank.question: Which word is a subordinating conjunction used to show a reason?
questionBank.explanation: 'Because' introduces a dependent clause that explains the reason for the main clause.

questionBank.question: Choose the correctly punctuated sentence:
options: Although it was cold we went for a walk.
Although it was cold, we went for a walk.
We went for a walk, although it was cold.
Both B and C are correct, but B is the standard 'start-sentence' rule.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both B and C are correct, but B is the standard 'start-sentence' rule.
questionBank.explanation: When 'Although' starts a sentence, a comma is required after the dependent clause.

questionBank.question: Which subordinator shows that one thing happened at the same time as another?
questionBank.explanation: 'While' is used to show two actions happening simultaneously.

questionBank.question: Complete the sentence: '___ you study hard, you will pass the exam.'
questionBank.explanation: 'If' introduces a condition necessary for the result in the main clause.

questionBank.question: What is the function of 'Unless'?
options: To show a negative condition (if not)
questionBank.correctAnswer: To show a negative condition (if not)
questionBank.explanation: 'Unless' means 'if... not'. Example: 'Unless it rains (if it doesn't rain), we will go.'

questionBank.question: Identify the subordinator in: 'Since the technology is new, it has some bugs.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Since' here acts as a subordinator meaning 'because'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence uses 'Until' correctly?
options: I will wait until you arrive.
Until you arrive, I will wait.
I will wait, until you arrive.
questionBank.explanation: 'Until' indicates time up to a specific point and follows standard comma rules.

questionBank.question: Choose the subordinator that shows a surprise or contrast:
questionBank.explanation: 'Even though' (and 'Although') introduces a fact that makes the main clause surprising.

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'Because, I was tired, I went to bed early.'
questionBank.explanation: You should never put a comma immediately after a subordinating conjunction.

questionBank.question: Which word can replace 'Because' at the start of a sentence?
questionBank.explanation: These are all formal subordinators used to introduce a cause.

questionBank.question: Complete the 'Condition' sentence: 'I won't go ___ you come with me.'
questionBank.explanation: This means 'I won't go if you don't come with me'.

questionBank.question: Where is the dependent clause? 'Whenever it rains, the roof leaks.'
questionBank.explanation: The dependent clause is the one that contains the subordinating conjunction.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is correctly punctuated in the 'middle position'?
options: He failed the test, because he didn't study.
He failed the test because he didn't study.
He failed, the test because he didn't study.
Because he didn't study he failed the test.
questionBank.correctAnswer: He failed the test because he didn't study.
questionBank.explanation: Standard 'because' in the middle does not require a comma.

questionBank.question: What does 'Whereas' show?
questionBank.explanation: 'Whereas' is used to compare two things that are different (e.g., 'Apples are red, whereas lemons are yellow').

questionBank.question: Choose the best subordinator: '___ I finish my work, I will call you.'
questionBank.explanation: 'As soon as' shows an immediate time relationship.

questionBank.question: Is this a complete sentence? 'Because the weather was bad.'
options: Yes, if it has a period.
questionBank.explanation: A dependent clause cannot stand alone. It needs an independent clause to complete the thought.

questionBank.question: Which of these shows a 'purpose'?
questionBank.explanation: 'So that' and 'In order that' explain the goal or purpose of an action.

questionBank.question: Identify the contrast subordinator: 'She is very kind, ___ her brother is quite rude.'
questionBank.explanation: 'While' can be used to show contrast between two people or things.

questionBank.question: Complete the 'Time' clause: '___ the movie ended, we went home.'
questionBank.explanation: 'After' shows the chronological sequence.

questionBank.question: Which sentence uses 'Even if' correctly?
options: Even if it rains, the game will continue.
The game will continue, even if it rains.
Even if, it rains the game will continue.
questionBank.explanation: 'Even if' follows the standard subordination comma rules.

questionBank.question: What is the formal version of 'If'?
questionBank.explanation: 'Provided that' or 'Providing that' are formal subordinators for conditions.

questionBank.question: Identify the subordinator: 'Now that the project is finished, we can relax.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Now that' is a subordinator showing a new situation that causes a result.

questionBank.question: Choose the best subordinator for a Task 1 comparison: 'The price rose in 2010 ___ it fell in 2015.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Whereas' is the elite-level choice for comparing different data points.

questionBank.question: Which word is NOT a subordinating conjunction?
questionBank.explanation: 'However' is a transitional adverb (Topic 16). It usually starts a new sentence with a comma.

questionBank.question: Complete: '___ you have any questions, please let me know.'
questionBank.explanation: Introduces a condition.

questionBank.question: Where should the comma go? 'Since you are here we should start.'
questionBank.explanation: Comma separates the dependent clause from the independent clause.

questionBank.question: Which of these shows a 'concession'?
questionBank.explanation: A concession admits a fact that contrasts with the main idea.

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'I won't leave, until I see him.'
questionBank.explanation: When the subordinator is in the middle, we usually don't use a comma.

questionBank.question: What does 'As long as' mean?
questionBank.explanation: 'As long as' shows a condition that must remain true for the result to happen.

questionBank.question: Choose the best formal combination: '___ the costs are high, the benefits are greater.'
questionBank.explanation: 'While' and 'Although' are excellent for contrasting two complex ideas in one sentence.

levels.title: L1 Traps (The Double-Signal Error)
levels.miniLesson:
⚠️ Level 2: The One-Driver Rule

In English, you must choose either Subordination (Because/Although) or Coordination (So/But). You can never use both in the same sentence.

  • ❌ Because it was raining, so we stayed home.
  • ✅ Because it was raining, we stayed home.
  • ✅ It was raining, so we stayed home.
  • ❌ Although he is rich, but he is unhappy.
  • ✅ Although he is rich, he is unhappy.
  • ✅ He is rich, but he is unhappy.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
options: Because I was late, so I missed the bus.
Because I was late, I missed the bus.
I was late, because I missed the bus.
So I missed the bus because I was late.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Because I was late, I missed the bus.
questionBank.explanation: You cannot use 'Because' and 'so' together. You must choose one.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'Although she is smart, but she makes many mistakes.'
questionBank.explanation: Starting with 'Although' already shows contrast. Adding 'but' is redundant and a grammar error.

questionBank.question: Fix the sentence: 'Because the internet was down, so I couldn't send the email.'
options: The internet was down, so I couldn't send the email.
Because the internet was down I couldn't send the email.
The internet was down because I couldn't send the email.
Both A and B are correct fixes.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are correct fixes.
questionBank.explanation: A uses coordination (so); B uses subordination (Because). Both are correct English structures.

questionBank.question: Which of these is a common 'Japanese logic' error?
options: If it rains, I will stay home.
Though it is hard, but I will try.
I will try though it is hard.
It is hard, but I will try.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Though it is hard, but I will try.
questionBank.explanation: Japanese speakers often mirror the 'Non-temo... demo' structure by using 'Though' and 'but' together.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct contrast sentence:
options: While I like tea, but I don't like coffee.
While I like tea, I don't like coffee.
I like tea, while I don't like coffee.
Both B and C are correct.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both B and C are correct.
questionBank.explanation: 'While' replaces 'but' as the signal word. You don't need both.

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'Since the price is high, so few people buy it.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Since' functions like 'Because'. Using 'so' in the second clause is a double-signal error.

questionBank.question: Which sentence follows the 'One-Driver' rule?
options: Even though I'm tired, but I have to work.
I'm tired, but I have to work.
Even though I'm tired, I have to work.
Both B and C are correct.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both B and C are correct.
questionBank.explanation: You can use either 'Even though' or 'but', but never both at once.

questionBank.question: Identify the redundant word: 'Because the car was old, so it broke down often.'
questionBank.explanation: Removing 'so' fixes the sentence structure.

questionBank.question: How would you correctly rewrite: 'Although it's far, but I want to go.'?
options: Although it's far, I want to go.
It's far, but I want to go.
Because it's far, I want to go.
Both A and B are correct fixes.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are correct fixes.
questionBank.explanation: These remove the double contrast signal.

questionBank.question: Which subordinator is often incorrectly paired with 'so' by ESL learners?
questionBank.explanation: All three show cause and are frequently (but wrongly) paired with the result marker 'so'.

questionBank.question: Is this correct? 'Unless you hurry, so you will be late.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Unless' is a subordinator. Adding 'so' creates a double-signal error.

questionBank.question: Choose the best academic result sentence:
options: Because taxes rose, so spending dropped.
Both B and C are correct.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both B and C are correct.
questionBank.explanation: B is more formal (subordination); C is standard (coordination).

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'While he is rich, but he lives simply.'
questionBank.explanation: 'While' provides the contrast; 'but' is not needed.

questionBank.question: Correct this: 'As it was a holiday, so the banks were closed.'
options: As it was a holiday, the banks were closed.
It was a holiday, so the banks were closed.
Because it was a holiday, so the banks were closed.
Both A and B are correct.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are correct.
questionBank.explanation: Removing one of the signals makes the sentence grammatical.

questionBank.question: What is the logic error in: 'Though he studied, but he failed.'?
questionBank.explanation: Using two contrast markers ('Though' and 'but') is a logical redundancy in English.

questionBank.question: Which sentence avoids the L1 trap?
options: Because it's expensive, so I won't buy it.
Since it's expensive, but I will buy it.
Though it's expensive, I will buy it.
Although it's expensive, but I'll buy it.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Though it's expensive, I will buy it.
questionBank.explanation: It uses a single subordinator ('Though') without a coordinating conjunction ('but').

questionBank.question: Identify the redundant signal: 'If you are tired, then so you should rest.'
questionBank.explanation: In an 'If... then' structure, adding 'so' is unnecessary and clunky.

questionBank.question: Fix: 'Even though she's young, but she is very wise.'
options: Even though she's young, she is very wise.
She's young, but she is very wise.
While she's young, but she is very wise.
questionBank.explanation: Single-signal structures are the only correct way.

questionBank.question: Which word should be deleted? 'Because the box was heavy, so he needed help.'
options: Both A and B are options for fixing it.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are options for fixing it.
questionBank.explanation: You can delete 'Because' OR delete 'so', but not both.

questionBank.question: Identify the grammar error: 'Whereas Japan is mountainous, but the Netherlands is flat.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Whereas' is a contrast subordinator. 'But' is a contrast coordinator. Only one is allowed.

questionBank.question: Which version is correct for a TOEFL essay?
options: Since pollution is rising, so we need stricter laws.
Since pollution is rising, we need stricter laws.
Pollution is rising, so we need stricter laws.
questionBank.explanation: B is formal; C is neutral. Both are correct.

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'Because he didn't check the map, and so he got lost.'
questionBank.explanation: Adding 'and so' after 'Because' is a triple-redundancy error!

questionBank.question: Why is 'Although he is poor, yet he is happy' acceptable in some literature but risky for students?
options: It is a 'double contrast' and often marked wrong on exams.
questionBank.explanation: It is an archaic structure. For IELTS/TOEFL, stick to the 'One-Driver' rule.

questionBank.question: Fix: 'Since you're here, so let's start.'
options: Because you're here, so let's start.
questionBank.explanation: Simple, single-signal fixes are best.

questionBank.question: Identify the incorrect contrast:
options: While I agree, I still have concerns.
I agree, but I still have concerns.
While I agree, but I still have concerns.
Although I agree, I still have concerns.
questionBank.correctAnswer: While I agree, but I still have concerns.
questionBank.explanation: Double-contrast error.

questionBank.question: What happens to the comma if you delete 'Because' in 'Because it's hot, I'm swimming'?
options: It is removed if you add 'so'.
questionBank.explanation: If you start with 'It's hot, so...', the comma is required before 'so'. If you just say 'It's hot. I'm swimming', the comma goes away.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct logic: '___ the movie was long, I enjoyed it.'
questionBank.explanation: The relationship is contrast (long movie vs. enjoyment), so 'Although' or 'While' is needed.

questionBank.question: Identify the error: 'As soon as he arrived, but the meeting started.'
questionBank.explanation: 'As soon as' is the time signal. 'But' makes no sense here and breaks the grammar.

questionBank.question: Correct this sentence: 'Because the deadline is near, so we must work faster.'
options: The deadline is near, so we must work faster.
Because the deadline is near, we must work faster.
As the deadline is near, we must work faster.
All of the above are correct.
questionBank.correctAnswer: All of the above are correct.
questionBank.explanation: All three eliminate the double-signal error.

questionBank.question: Final Rule Check: Can you ever use 'Because' and 'So' in the same sentence?
options: Yes, if they are in different clauses.
questionBank.explanation: In academic writing and standard exams, this is a major grammatical error.

levels.title: Application & Relative Clauses
levels.miniLesson:
📝 Level 3: The Embedded Detail

Relative clauses allow you to add detail without starting a new sentence. There are two types:

  • 🔍 Defining (No Commas): Essential info.
    The students who study hard pass. (Only the ones who study).
  • 💡 Non-Defining (Commas): Extra info.
    My brother, who lives in Tokyo, is a doctor. (You only have one brother; the location is just extra).

questionBank.question: Which sentence correctly uses a relative clause to combine: 'I have a friend. He lives in Osaka.'
options: I have a friend who he lives in Osaka.
I have a friend who lives in Osaka.
I have a friend lives in Osaka.
I have a friend, lives in Osaka.
questionBank.correctAnswer: I have a friend who lives in Osaka.
questionBank.explanation: The relative pronoun 'who' replaces the subject 'He'. You cannot use both in the same clause.

questionBank.question: Choose the correctly punctuated 'non-defining' clause:
options: The Amazon River which is in South America is very long.
The Amazon River, which is in South America, is very long.
The Amazon River, which is in South America is very long.
The Amazon River which is in South America, is very long.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The Amazon River, which is in South America, is very long.
questionBank.explanation: Since there is only one Amazon River, the location is extra info and needs commas on both sides.

questionBank.question: What is the error in: 'The book that I bought it yesterday is interesting.'
options: There should be a comma after 'book'.
questionBank.explanation: The relative pronoun 'that' already represents the 'book'. Adding 'it' creates a double-object error.

questionBank.question: Which relative pronoun is used for a place?
questionBank.explanation: 'Where' is used to define or provide extra info about a location.

questionBank.question: Identify the defining relative clause: 'Students who skip classes usually fail.'
questionBank.explanation: This clause defines which specific students we are talking about. No commas are used.

questionBank.question: Combine: 'The laptop is broken. I bought it last week.'
options: The laptop I bought last week is broken.
The laptop which I bought it last week is broken.
The laptop, that I bought last week, is broken.
The laptop what I bought last week is broken.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The laptop I bought last week is broken.
questionBank.explanation: In English, you can often drop the relative pronoun (that/which) if it is the object of the clause.

questionBank.question: Which is correct for a person's possession? 'The man ___ car was stolen called the police.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Whose' is the possessive relative pronoun for both people and things.

questionBank.question: Find the punctuation error: 'My mother who is a teacher, likes to read.'
questionBank.explanation: Non-defining clauses (extra info about a specific person) must be enclosed in commas: ', who is a teacher,'.

questionBank.question: Which relative pronoun can NEVER follow a comma?
questionBank.explanation: In academic English, 'that' is only for defining clauses (no commas). For non-defining, use 'which' or 'who'.

questionBank.question: Choose the best academic combination: 'The study was published in 2020. It explored the effects of caffeine.'
options: The study, which was published in 2020, explored the effects of caffeine.
The study published in 2020, explored the effects of caffeine.
The study which was published in 2020 explored the effects of caffeine.
Both A and B (B is a reduced relative clause).
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B (B is a reduced relative clause).
questionBank.explanation: A uses a full non-defining clause; B reduces it by removing 'which was'. Both are high-level academic structures.

questionBank.question: What does 'Whom' refer to?
questionBank.explanation: In formal English, 'whom' is used when the person is the object of the verb or preposition (e.g., 'To whom it may concern').

questionBank.question: Identify the trap: 'The reason why he left is a secret.'
questionBank.explanation: We can use 'why' or 'that' to introduce a relative clause describing a reason.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is correctly combined? 'Tokyo is a city. Millions of people live there.'
options: Tokyo is a city where millions of people live there.
Tokyo is a city where millions of people live.
Tokyo is a city which millions of people live.
Tokyo is a city millions of people live.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Tokyo is a city where millions of people live.
questionBank.explanation: When using 'where', you must remove the original place adverb 'there'.

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'The company, that produces these cars, is located in Nagoya.'
options: Both A and B are possible fixes.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are possible fixes.
questionBank.explanation: You can't use 'that' with commas. Either change it to 'which' OR remove the commas to make it a defining clause.

questionBank.question: Which is more formal? 'The person I talked to' or 'The person to whom I spoke'?
questionBank.explanation: Moving the preposition ('to') before 'whom' is the gold standard for academic formal register.

questionBank.question: Identify the 'Reduced' relative clause: 'The man sitting by the window is my uncle.'
questionBank.explanation: This is a reduction of 'who is sitting by the window'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence correctly defines a time? 'Summer is the season ___.'
questionBank.explanation: 'When' is the relative adverb for time.

questionBank.question: Is the comma necessary here? 'The car, that was stolen, was found.'
questionBank.explanation: 'That' is strictly for defining clauses without commas.

questionBank.question: Complete with 'Whose': 'I saw a house ___ roof was red.'
questionBank.explanation: Used for possession by an inanimate object (the house's roof).

questionBank.question: Identify the non-defining clause: 'My only sister, who lives in Paris, is visiting.'
questionBank.explanation: Since there is only one sister, the information about Paris is non-essential (extra).

questionBank.question: Which sentence has a 'Double Subject' error?
options: The movie which I saw it was great.
The movie I saw was great.
The movie which I saw was great.
The movie, which I saw, was great.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The movie which I saw it was great.
questionBank.explanation: 'Which' and 'it' both refer to the movie. Remove 'it'.

questionBank.question: Choose the formal relative pronoun: 'The policy, ___ was debated for months, failed.'
questionBank.explanation: With commas, you must use 'which' for things.

questionBank.question: Fix the error: 'He is the man whom lives next door.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Who' is the subject of the clause ('lives next door'). 'Whom' is only for objects.

questionBank.question: Identify the relative adverb: 'The village where I grew up is small.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Where' describes the village.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is correctly reduced? 'The books which were written by him are good.'
options: The books written by him are good.
The books writing by him are good.
The books were written by him are good.
The books by him are good.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The books written by him are good.
questionBank.explanation: You can reduce a passive relative clause by removing 'which were'.

questionBank.question: Where does the comma go? 'Mr. Smith who is my boss called me.'
questionBank.explanation: A specific name followed by extra info needs a comma sandwich: 'Mr. Smith, who is my boss, called me.'

questionBank.question: Choose the correct subordinator: 'The reason ___ she left remains unclear.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Why' is the relative adverb for reasons.

questionBank.question: Identify the trap: 'I saw the girl whose brother is famous.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Whose' is the correct possessive; 'who's' means 'who is' or 'who has'.

questionBank.question: Which sentence is a 'Comma Splice' error?
options: The car is old, it still runs.
The car, which is old, still runs.
The car is old; it still runs.
Although the car is old, it still runs.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The car is old, it still runs.
questionBank.explanation: Joining two independent sentences with only a comma is a major error. Options B, C, and D are correct ways to fix it.

questionBank.question: What is the primary function of relative clauses in academic writing?
options: To add precision and detail to nouns without repeating information.
To sound like a native speaker.
questionBank.correctAnswer: To add precision and detail to nouns without repeating information.
questionBank.explanation: Relative clauses are essential for the 'Lexical Resource' and 'Grammatical Range' scores in IELTS/TOEFL.

levels.title: IELTS/TOEFL Integration
levels.miniLesson:
📈 Level 4: The Synthesis Master

At the highest level, subordination is used for synthesis. You must master the 'Sentential Which' and 'Prepositional Relative Pronouns' to connect abstract ideas.

  • 🔗 Sentential 'Which': Refers to the whole previous clause.
    The birth rate fell, which caused concern for the government.
  • 🏛️ Prepositional Relative: For formal register.
    The theory to which I refer is quite complex.
  • ✂️ Reduced Clauses: For density.
    Research conducted in 2020 (reduced from 'which was conducted') is vital.

questionBank.question: Which sentence uses 'which' to refer to the entire preceding idea?
options: The company launched a new product which was very successful.
The company launched a new product, which was very successful.
The company launched a new product, which surprised its competitors.
The company which launched the product is very successful.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The company launched a new product, which surprised its competitors.
questionBank.explanation: In this sentence, 'which' refers to the *action* of the company launching a product, not just the 'product' itself.

questionBank.question: Select the most formal version of this sentence: 'This is the issue I am worried about.'
options: This is the issue which I am worried about.
This is the issue about which I am concerned.
This is the issue that I am worried about.
About which issue I am concerned is this.
questionBank.correctAnswer: This is the issue about which I am concerned.
questionBank.explanation: Moving the preposition ('about') before the relative pronoun ('which') is a hallmark of high-level academic register.

questionBank.question: Identify the 'Reduced Relative Clause' best suited for a Task 1 overview:
options: The figures which are showing an upward trend are clear.
The figures showing an upward trend are clear.
The figures show an upward trend, which is clear.
questionBank.explanation: B uses a reduced active clause (showing); C uses a sentential relative clause (which is clear). Both are elite structures.

questionBank.question: What is the error in: 'Not only the government failed to act, which led to a crisis.'
options: Both A and B are necessary to fix the main clause.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are necessary to fix the main clause.
questionBank.explanation: 'Not only' at the start requires inversion (did the government fail) and a matching 'but also' later.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct prepositional relative: 'The environment ___ we live is changing.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Where' and 'in which' are both correct, but 'in which' is preferred in formal academic writing.

questionBank.question: Identify the sentential relative: 'Global warming is accelerating, which many scientists believe is irreversible.'
options: which many scientists believe is irreversible
questionBank.correctAnswer: which many scientists believe is irreversible
questionBank.explanation: This 'which' refers to the fact that global warming is accelerating.

questionBank.question: Which Task 1 sentence effectively uses a relative clause for comparison?
options: Sales in 2010 were 50%, which was double the figure for 2005.
Sales in 2010 were 50% which was double the figure for 2005.
Sales in 2010 were 50%, that was double the figure for 2005.
Sales in 2010 were 50% and this was double the figure for 2005.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Sales in 2010 were 50%, which was double the figure for 2005.
questionBank.explanation: This uses a non-defining relative clause to provide a comparative analysis of the data.

questionBank.question: Fix the error: 'The participants were divided into two groups, each of whom received a different treatment.'
options: Change 'each of whom' to 'every of whom'.
questionBank.explanation: Since 'groups' are inanimate entities/collections, 'which' is used. 'Whom' is only for people.

questionBank.question: Which sentence shows 'Nominalization' through subordination?
options: The fact that the population is aging presents many challenges.
Because the population is aging, it presents challenges.
The population is aging, and this presents challenges.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The fact that the population is aging presents many challenges.
questionBank.explanation: Using 'The fact that...' turns a whole clause into a single noun phrase subject.

questionBank.question: Where should the comma go in this sentential relative? 'He forgot my birthday which was very upsetting.'
questionBank.explanation: A sentential relative clause must *always* be separated from the main clause by a comma.

questionBank.question: Which relative pronoun is used to show a 'manner' in formal writing?
options: The way in which it works...
questionBank.correctAnswer: The way in which it works...
questionBank.explanation: 'The way how' is ungrammatical. 'The way in which' is the formal academic standard.

questionBank.question: Identify the correctly reduced passive relative clause:
options: The law passing last year is controversial.
The law passed last year is controversial.
The law which passing last year is controversial.
The law which passed last year is controversial.
questionBank.correctAnswer: The law passed last year is controversial.
questionBank.explanation: This is a reduction of 'The law *that was* passed last year'.

questionBank.question: Complete the Task 1 comparison: 'Production levels reached 100 units, ___ for the rest of the year.'
questionBank.explanation: 'After which' is a high-level relative phrase used to show a sequence in data trends.

questionBank.question: Which of these is a 'Fragment' error?
options: Although the costs were high, the project continued.
The project continued although the costs were high.
Even if the costs were high.
questionBank.explanation: A dependent clause without an independent clause is a major grammar error (Sentence Fragment).

questionBank.question: Which formal subordinator shows 'Extent'?
questionBank.explanation: These are all used to qualify an argument by showing to what degree it is true.

questionBank.question: Fix the error: 'The city, that I grew up in, has changed a lot.'
questionBank.explanation: You cannot use 'that' with commas. If you want commas, use 'which'; if you want 'that', remove commas.

questionBank.question: Which sentence effectively uses 'whose' for an inanimate object?
options: The company whose headquarters are in New York...
The company the headquarters of which are in New York...
Both are correct, but A is more natural.
Both are correct, but B is more formal.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both are correct, but B is more formal.
questionBank.explanation: 'The headquarters of which' is very formal; 'whose' is common and acceptable for objects.

questionBank.question: Identify the 'Double Relative' trap: 'The reason why he left that I heard was strange.'
options: Should be 'The reason why he left, which I heard, was strange.'
Should be 'The reason that he left was strange.'
questionBank.explanation: Nesting multiple relative clauses ('why' and 'that') without commas makes the sentence difficult to read.

questionBank.question: Choose the correct subordinator for a 'Condition' in Task 2: '___ governments invest in education, the economy will not improve.'
questionBank.explanation: The context requires a negative condition: 'Unless (if they don't) invest, it won't improve'.

questionBank.question: Which relative pronoun is used to refer back to a whole list? 'Books, pens, and paper, ___ are necessary for the exam...'
questionBank.explanation: 'All of which' (or 'some of which') is the standard way to refer to a previously mentioned set.

questionBank.question: Find the error: 'The report provides data regarding to which countries produced the most oil.'
questionBank.explanation: It should be 'data regarding which countries...' or 'data as to which countries...'. 'Regarding' is a preposition and shouldn't have 'to'.

questionBank.question: Which of these is a 'Relative Clause of Result'?
options: The fire spread quickly, with the result that the building was destroyed.
The fire spread quickly, which resulted in the building being destroyed.
The fire spread quickly, so that the building was destroyed.
questionBank.explanation: These are all high-level ways to subordinate a result to a cause.

questionBank.question: Identify the correctly punctuated 'concession' clause:
options: In spite of the weather we went hiking.
In spite of the weather, we went hiking.
Despite the weather, we went hiking.
questionBank.explanation: Introductory prepositional phrases of concession require a comma.

questionBank.question: Choose the best formal subordinator for 'Instead of':
questionBank.explanation: These all elevate the register of your comparisons in an essay.

questionBank.question: Which is correct? 'The person ___ I am talking to is my boss.'
options: All of the above (though 'to whom' is most formal).
questionBank.correctAnswer: All of the above (though 'to whom' is most formal).
questionBank.explanation: In standard English all are used, but 'to whom' is the academic gold standard.

questionBank.question: Identify the trap in: 'No matter how hard he tried, but he failed.'
options: Both A and B are options.
questionBank.correctAnswer: Both A and B are options.
questionBank.explanation: This is another double-signal error. You can't use 'No matter' (subordination) and 'but' (coordination) together.

questionBank.question: Which relative pronoun is used to define an 'area' of research?
options: The field in which I work...
questionBank.explanation: Abstract 'places' like a field of study can take 'where' or 'in which'.

questionBank.question: Identify the formal way to say 'If': '___ that the weather is good, we will go.'
questionBank.explanation: 'Providing that' and 'Provided that' are formal equivalents of 'If'.

questionBank.question: What is the function of 'Inasmuch as'?
options: To show a reason/extent (Because/To the degree that).
questionBank.correctAnswer: To show a reason/extent (Because/To the degree that).
questionBank.explanation: It is a very formal way to introduce a justifying fact.

questionBank.question: Final Rule: Why is subordination the key to a Band 7.0+ score?
options: It shows you can handle complex logic and grammar.
It allows for precise comparisons and cause-and-effect links.
questionBank.explanation: Subordination is the primary driver of 'Grammatical Range' and 'Cohesion' in advanced English exams.
